title: 20 Hidar — The First Entry into Egypt & The Assembly
narrative:
title": "20 Hidar — The First Entry into Egypt & The Assembly

narrative": "title\": \"20 Hidar — The First Entry into Egypt & The Assembly

narrative\": \"Hidar

The First Entry of Christ into Egypt (Monthly Commemoration)

St. Anianus, Second Bishop of Alexandria

St. Gregory of Decapolis, Monastic

The twentieth day of Hidar carries the monthly commemoration of the First Entry into Egypt, and in the apostolic month the Flight into Egypt takes on a specific missiological quality that the other months' versions of this feast do not emphasize in the same way. The Holy Family in Egypt was the apostolic community before the apostolic community existed — the incarnate Word physically present in the land of the Pharaohs, the divine presence inhabiting the Egyptian soil. The Coptic tradition has always read this event as the foundation of Egypt's Christian identity: the Word who would one day send the apostles to Egypt first came Himself, as a refugee child, sanctifying the land with His presence before any missionary foot had touched it. In the apostolic month of Hidar, the First Entry into Egypt is the testimony that the apostolic mission is always preceded by the divine presence — that the community goes to places where God has already arrived.

St. Anianus was the second Bishop of Alexandria — the man who received the apostolic commission from St. Mark the Evangelist and who led the Alexandrian Church through its first generation of growth. His place in the tradition is the place of the faithful successor: the one who receives the commission from its originator and maintains it across the transition between the founding generation and the second generation. In the apostolic month of Hidar, Anianus is the testimony that the vesting is transferable — that the laying on of hands through which Mark vested Anianus is the same gesture through which every subsequent episcopal vesting has distributed the apostolic commission. The continuous tradition of ordination is the physical lineage of the apostolic vesting, the hands that have carried the commission from the Upper Room to the present day without interruption.

St. Gregory of Decapolis was a ninth-century Byzantine monastic whose journeys — from the monasteries of Asia Minor to Constantinople to Rome — made him one of the great advocates of the icon-venerating orthodox position during the final stages of the iconoclast controversy. His apostolic vesting was a vesting for the specific mission of the theological ambassador: the monk who travels between communities to communicate the doctrinal position they need to hear, to strengthen the orthodox faith of communities isolated by their situation, and to build the network of conciliar understanding that sustained the Church through the controversy's resolution. In Hidar's assembly, Gregory of Decapolis is the apostolic traveler — the one whose commission requires him to be perpetually on the road, carrying the truth of the common faith between communities that need to hear each other.

The First Entry into Egypt, Anianus, and Gregory of Decapolis together on the twentieth day present the apostolic vesting in its pre-missionary, successional, and ambassadorial expressions. The Holy Family precedes the mission into Egypt, sanctifying the ground. Anianus receives the succession and stabilizes the second generation. Gregory travels between the churches, strengthening the bonds of the common faith. All three are forms of the apostolic commission operating in the world — the divine presence going ahead, the human institution sustaining the continuity, the individual apostolic traveler maintaining the connection. The community in Hidar's twentieth day is shown the full range of what it means to be sent.
